paragraphs.body: My dad has taught me almost everything I know and embody today. I am a living embodiment of him in a sense. Technically, I am literally a part of him, but outside of that I try to be as close to him as possible.

Sports is a very important topic in my life. I can credit all of my sports knowledge and ability to him. I remember the first time I was watched a full sporting event with my dad. It was at my old house, I was finally allowed to stay up late enough to watch a Pittsburgh Pirates game with him. My favorite player was Andrew McCutchen, which is crazy to think about because he still plays on the Pirates today. Anyways, I remember exactly where I was sitting on the old couch and where he was too. I was sitting on the left side and was short enough to be half standing and leaning onto the top cushion of the couch and he was sitting on the right side as we both watched the television.

I also remember the first athletic event I was a part of with him, or at least I remember many of them from a long time ago. He was my coach for a while when I was really small, and he ever since showed up to every single game he possibly could of. To give both of my parents credit, I don’t think I ever played a game of any sport that at least one of them wasn’t at. The very first team I remember being on was the A’s, named after the Oakland A’s, at Foxpoint Baseball. My love for sports only grew from there, and I only learned more and more from him as time went on. He still teaches me how to be better at sports to this day.

Religion is also a big part of my life. Ever since I can remember, he has been taking me and my brother to Church every weekend. As a kid it was so boring and I remember complaining and zoning out when we got there, but I have learned to like it. It brings peace into my life.

It is hard to describe every detail of how he made me the way I am because it just happens over time. But I can try to explain at least some of it. He taught me my manners, respect, discipline, and patience. I am more that grateful that I learned these things from him because he is one of the best people I’ve met at all of these things.

annotations.text: He taught me my manners, respect, discipline, and patience.
annotations.comment: These would be ideas you might explore if you choose to do a revision. Remember that an essay answers the "so what" question and should have purpose for an audience larger than yourself.

paragraphs.body: A wish: I wish I could sit in front of a television screen and watch his whole childhood to the point where he is at now. He has told me stories about what his life was like, but I wish so badly I’d be able to actually see it. It festinated me. I want to be able to see the things he talked about and heard and felt. Honestly, life seemed so much better back then in a lot of ways. Social media didn’t exist, so you only met people naturally. The amount of stuff you could get away with made for so much more fun. One of my favorite stories he tells me is during the summer him and his friends in the neighborhood would fill a giant cooler with water. They’d play basketball all day every day and leave the cooler there all week and drink from it until they had to refill it at the end of the week. I can’t explain in words how badly I wish I could’ve been there and done that. There are also so many other stories similar to that I wish I could have partaken in. Outside of just the fun though, I’d want to see core memories and events in his life. What made him the man he is today.

annotations.text: One of my favorite stories he tells me is during the summer him and his friends in the neighborhood would fill a giant cooler with water. They’d play basketball all day every day and leave the cooler there all week and drink from it until they had to refill it at the end of the week. I can’t explain in words how badly I wish I could’ve been there and done that. There are also so many other stories similar to that I wish I could have partaken in. Outside of just the fun though, I’d want to see core memories and events in his life. What made him the man he is today.
annotations.comment: Yes! Can you imagine developing an essay layered with all of the "old-school" moments of fun your dad had that you wish you could also have? The whole essay could be about what is lost because of the technology that has interceded.

annotations.text: Honestly, life seemed so much better back then in a lot of ways. Social media didn’t exist, so you only met people naturally. The amount of stuff you could get away with made for so much more fun.
annotations.comment: This is another great idea you could explore and develop!

paragraphs.body: Some fathers are distant, and some aren’t even there. Others can be tyrants, and others can be ghosts or shells of what they wanted to be. My dad isn’t any of these things. He is my biggest role model. Everything he is has made me a better person and will always leave me with my wishes that I am fascinated by, because I’m always going to want to know exactly how we got to where we are now.

annotations.text: Some fathers are distant, and some aren’t even there. Others can be tyrants, and others can be ghosts or shells of what they wanted to be.
annotations.comment: More great ideas! You could develop a whole essay around this notion of what makes a "good" father, too!

Hello, This essay makes your relationship very clear from the opening sentence to the final line. The central focusing idea of this piece is clearly that your father is one of, if not the, most important role model in your life, and this is supported by your big ideas centering on your celebration of so many of his specific traits and your overall feelings towards him. The words that stand out the most to me are baseball, cooler, ghost, shell. All of these words open up for me (i.e. “worlds within words”) and present opportunities for deeper exploration and discovery. I’m especially drawn to the vicarious memory you have of your dad playing basketball all day every day, which makes me wonder about what you think we may have lost—what you may have lost—because the one-on-one experiences, the camaraderie, the simple fun of summer has been replaced by technology (or something else?). I also see repetitions in terms of sports—e.g. baseball—and the closeness between you and your father because of those sporting events What I admire most about your essay is the scene (and also the other sports moments) that I mentioned above. The feeling that moment evokes is palpable. You create a feeling of nostalgia this is, at once, bittersweet and enticing. It feels like a moment that I, too, wish I could live in. The primary audience of this essay is probably your father, as it seems to be a celebration of him throughout. But I can also see a way in which this could become an essay for a larger audience, too. There are so many opportunities for greater focus and exploration (see my notes in-text). The essay is a celebration, not only of your father, but also of the life he led—the life you also wish you could experience—and I think a lot of people would share that nostalgia for something they never had with you. What I learned from reading your essay is that you are a multi-faceted, deep-thinker. You value having fun and enjoying life, and have dedicated a lot of your mind and your time to celebrating the people and experiences that bring you joy in life. It’s really admirable that you can take this relationship with your father and turn it into an essay like this. I am also quite taken with the ways you begin to weave several ideas in a way that reveals meaning and friction. You could get even more intentional with those compelling moments and create an essay with additional nuance and layers (is there a fragrance metaphor here?). It was a pleasure reading your essay and getting to know you better through your own experiences and observations. Thanks,
